did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a hybrid deep learning model for skin disease classification. It used ResNet-50 for deep feature extraction and a modified DenseNet-50 for classification, with LLM-supported refinement during model tuning. The system was trained on dermoscopic images of seven skin disease classes (including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma) using TensorFlow, categorical cross-entropy loss, and the Adam optimizer, and deployed via a backend server for cloud-based inference.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a hybrid deep learning model (ResNet-50 and DenseNet-50) for skin disease classification. It details specific methods including dataset preprocessing, model architecture, training parameters (80 epochs, Adam optimizer), and performance evaluation on seven skin disease classes, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Classification Using Neural Networks
journal: International Research Journal on Advanced Engineering Hub
brief_summary: This study developed a hybrid deep learning model for automated skin disease classification to address challenges in early detection and diagnostic variability. The system integrates ResNet-50 for deep feature extraction with a modified DenseNet-50 for classification, utilizing LLM-supported refinement during model tuning. It was trained on publicly available dermoscopic datasets covering seven clinically relevant skin disease classes, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on held-out validation data, with the model deployed via a backend server for cloud-based inference on an Android application. The results demonstrated stable performance across most conditions, with particularly high reliability for vascular lesions and dermatofibromas, though melanoma and benign keratosis-like lesions showed lower scores due to visual complexity. The authors conclude that this hybrid pipeline serves as a fast, scalable, and low-cost tool for preliminary screening, supporting both clinical environments and remote users.

all_task_types.target: Seven clinically relevant skin disease classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses with intraepithelial carcinoma, melanocytic nevi, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, and benign keratosis-like lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images into seven discrete diagnostic categories using a hybrid ResNet-50 and modified DenseNet-50 architecture. The final layer produced probabilities for the seven target diseases, and the system delivered class predictions and confidence values.

datasets_used.name: Publicly available dermoscopic datasets
datasets_used.description: The paper states that dermoscopic skin-lesion images were collected from publicly available medical datasets covering seven diagnostic classes. Specific dataset names (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000) are not provided in the text.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid ResNet-50 and modified DenseNet-50 model.
use.validation: Used for validation (10% split) and performance evaluation.
use.testing: Used for held-out validation data evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance across the seven skin disease categories.
To prevent overfitting during the training process.
To improve model generalization and reduce class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix analysis on held-out validation data.

Publicly available dermoscopic datasets.metric: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrix

Hybrid ResNet-50 + Modified DenseNet-50.notes: The paper reports stable performance across all seven skin disease categories with balanced precision and recall. Specific numerical values for accuracy, precision, recall, or F1-score are not provided in the text. The model achieved high reliability for vascular lesions and dermatofibromas. Melanoma and benign keratosis-like lesions showed comparatively lower scores due to visual complexity. Inference latency was approximately 12–15 seconds.

Publicly available dermoscopic datasets.notes: The paper does not provide specific quantitative metric values (e.g., 95.2% accuracy). It describes the results qualitatively as 'stable', 'balanced', and 'high reliability' for specific classes, while noting lower scores for others. Table 1 lists experimental parameters but not the final performance metrics.

conclusions: The hybrid deep learning pipeline integrating ResNet-50 for feature extraction and a modified DenseNet-50 for classification serves as a fast, low-cost, and scalable tool for preliminary skin disease assessment.
The system supports both clinical environments and remote users by delivering confidence-based predictions suitable for real-time screening applications.
The model achieved stable performance with balanced precision and recall across seven skin disease categories, with particularly high reliability for vascular lesions and dermatofibromas.
Melanoma and benign keratosis-like lesions showed comparatively lower performance scores due to their greater visual complexity in dermoscopic imaging.
The combination of ResNet-50 and DenseNet-50 improves feature discrimination and classification stability by combining deep residual features with dense connectivity.
The system is designed as an educational and supportive screening tool to encourage timely medical consultation, rather than to replace professional diagnosis.
Cloud-based deployment via a backend server avoids mobile storage limitations but introduces a dependency on internet connectivity, which may affect performance in low-network environments.
Further dataset expansion to include more skin tones and rare lesions, along with clinical validation, is required for practical medical deployment.
important_remarks: The paper does not report specific quantitative performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score values), describing results only qualitatively as 'stable' or 'high reliability'.
The specific names of the publicly available dermoscopic datasets used for training and validation are not disclosed in the text.
The study lacks external validation; the model was evaluated only on held-out validation data from the source datasets, with no mention of independent test sets or multi-center validation.
There is no reporting of demographic diversity metrics, such as skin tone distribution (Fitzpatrick scale), which is a critical factor for dermatological AI generalizability.
The system relies on cloud-based inference via a backend server, introducing a dependency on internet connectivity that may limit utility in low-network environments.
Inference latency was reported as approximately 12–15 seconds, which is noted as typical for a prototype stage but may be suboptimal for real-time clinical workflows.
The paper acknowledges that the system is intended for preliminary screening and educational support, explicitly stating it is not a replacement for professional diagnosis.
Performance was notably lower for melanoma and benign keratosis-like lesions due to visual complexity, highlighting potential limitations in handling diagnostically challenging cases.
